title: Choice and long text
checkLabels.t3: There is a choice from a list
checkLabels.t4: The list has at least two options
checkLabels.t9: There is a large text field
checkLabels.t11: The large field has a name
why.empty: The editor is empty. You deleted the form too.
why.untouched: This is exactly the code I gave you. Nothing has been changed.
hint2.untouched: Two of the three fields need to change their tag. The name field stays as it is.
why.not-closed: A tag is open and never closed. <textarea> and <select> have closing tags, unlike <input>.
hint2.not-closed: <select> wants </select>. <option> wants </option>. <textarea> wants </textarea>. Three new closing tags in one lesson.
why.lost: You deleted part of the form. The three labels, the name field and the button all stay — the task is about the other two fields.
why.no-select: Which bike — still a free field. You know exactly what the possible answers are. Give them. <select> is a list a person chooses from.
hint2.no-select: The field with name="model" is the one that becomes a list.
hint3.no-select: A similar case: <select id="city" name="city"><option value="sofia">Sofia</option></select>.
hint4.no-select: Swap the <input> for a <select> and do not forget the </select>. The id and name attributes stay.
why.no-option: The list is there, but it is empty, or it has only one option. A list with one option is not a choice. Each possibility is written with <option>.
hint2.no-option: The two bikes from the whole course: mountain and city. So two <option>.
hint4.no-option: Every <option> sits INSIDE the <select>, between the opening and closing tag.
why.empty-option: There is an empty option. An empty <option> shows as a blank line in the list — a person does not know what they are choosing.
hint2.empty-option: The word a person sees sits between the tags: <option value="city">City bike</option>.
why.no-value: An option with no value, or an empty one. Then the visible text arrives at the server — or nothing does. value is what gets sent.
hint2.no-value: value is an attribute on <option>, inside the opening tag.
hint3.no-value: The person sees Mountain bike, and mountain arrives at the server. The two can differ — and usually do.
why.select-not-tied: The list has no id, or it is not the one the label is looking for. The label Which bike points at model — it survives from the last lesson and you should not break it.
hint2.select-not-tied: The old <input> had id="model". The new <select> inherits it.
why.select-no-name: The list has no name. Without a name the choice is not sent — as if it were not there.
hint2.select-no-name: name goes on the <select>, not on the <option>. The <option> has a value.
why.no-textarea: The message is still a one-line field. Like handing someone a business card to write a letter on. <textarea> is the big field.
hint2.no-textarea: The field with name="message" is the one that becomes big.
hint3.no-textarea: A similar case: <textarea id="comment" name="comment" rows="5"></textarea>.
hint4.no-textarea: The closing tag sits right against the opening one. Leave a blank line between them and it lands inside the field.
why.textarea-not-tied: The large field has no id, or it is not the one the label is looking for. The label Your message points at message.
hint2.textarea-not-tied: The old <input> had id="message". The new <textarea> inherits it.
why.textarea-no-name: The large field has no name. Whatever a person writes in it is not sent.
why.still-input: An old field is still there. You are not adding a list NEXT to the text field — you are REPLACING it. Only one <input> remains: the one for the name.
hint2.still-input: Count the <input> tags. There should be one. Two or three means an old field is still standing.
blocks.0.text: The form has three fields and all of them are <input type="text">. Meaning: write whatever you like.
blocks.1.text: But you know which bikes there are. Why let the person spell them out? They will get it wrong, write a different name, capitalise it, and you will be the one sorting it out later.
blocks.4.text: <select> is a list. <option> is each possibility. value is what gets sent — the text between the tags is what the person sees.
blocks.5.text: The two can differ. The person sees Mountain bike, and mountain arrives at the server.
blocks.8.text: <textarea> is for free text longer than one line. A message, a comment, a description.
blocks.9.text: rows says how many lines tall it starts. The person can drag it bigger themselves.
blocks.11.text: Notice something: <textarea> has a closing tag, and <input> does not.
blocks.12.text: And another thing: its starting content is written BETWEEN the tags, not in an attribute. With <input> it is value="…". Here — just text inside.
blocks.13.text: That is why the closing tag sits right against the opening one: <textarea></textarea>. Leave a blank line between them and it lands in the field, and the person starts typing on a blank second line.
blocks.14.text: A tiny detail everyone discovers the hard way.
blocks.16.text: Do you know the answers in advance — <select>. Do you not, and they are short — <input>. Do you not, and they are long — <textarea>.
blocks.17.text: Your turn. Make the bike choice a list, and the message a large field. The old fields do not stay — they get replaced. And mind the ids: the labels from the last lesson are looking for them.
